blocks.text: Chapter 1.
Life Is Poker, Not Chess
The ideas in 30 seconds

We routinely judge decisions by their outcomes — a bias poker players call resulting — even though a good decision can produce a bad result and a bad decision can produce a good one whenever luck is involved.
Life resembles poker, not chess: outcomes are driven by hidden information and luck as well as skill, so the better decision-maker can still lose any given hand.
John von Neumann built game theory on poker, not chess, because real-world decisions involve deception, incomplete information, and guessing what others will do.
Our brains evolved for certainty and quick pattern-matching (Kahneman's System 1), not for probabilistic reasoning; hindsight bias makes every outcome look inevitable after the fact.
"Wrong" needs a probabilistic redefinition: a decision that loses is not automatically a mistake — it may simply be the low-probability branch of a well-placed bet.
Getting comfortable saying "I'm not sure" is not weakness; it is the most accurate description of the world and the foundation of every better decision that follows.
The Worst Call in Super Bowl History Was a Good Bet

Super Bowl XLIX, February 2015: the Seattle Seahawks trail New England by four with 26 seconds left, second down on the Patriots' one-yard line, one timeout remaining. Everyone expects a handoff to Marshawn Lynch, the league's premier power runner. Instead, coach Pete Carroll calls a pass. Malcolm Butler intercepts it, New England wins, and by morning the headlines have crowned it the worst call in Super Bowl history.

Annie Duke's opening move is to ask a question almost no commentator asked: was the call bad, or was the outcome bad? Statistically, the pass was defensible. In the prior fifteen seasons, the interception rate on goal-line passes was about 2%. A pass also stops the clock if incomplete, effectively buying Seattle three shots at the end zone (pass, then two runs) with only one timeout, instead of two. Carroll didn't make a terrible decision; he made a reasonable decision that landed on the unlucky 2% branch. Had the pass scored, he would have been hailed as a genius for out-thinking Belichick.

Judging a decision by its result is what poker players call resulting, and Duke shows it operating far beyond football. When she asks executive audiences to name their best and worst decisions of the past year, they reliably name their best and worst outcomes. One CEO called firing a company president his worst decision ever — yet on examination, the process had been careful and well-reasoned; the outcome was simply bad. Conflating the two teaches exactly the wrong lessons.

The correction is not "ignore outcomes" but "loosen the link." Decision quality and outcome quality are correlated only over the long run. In any single instance, luck sits between them — and a thinking-in-bets mindset starts by refusing to let one result stand in for the whole probability distribution it came from.
Why Life Looks Like Poker, Not Chess

We casually treat chess as the emblem of strategic genius, but chess is the wrong model for life. Chess is a game of complete information: both players see the entire board, nothing is hidden, and there is no luck. If Garry Kasparov loses, it is because someone outplayed him — outcomes map cleanly onto decision quality, so working backward from a bad result to find the bad move is legitimate.

Poker is the opposite. Cards are concealed, future deals are random, and opponents actively deceive. A world-class player making excellent decisions can lose all night to a tourist making awful ones. Duke recounts players like the fabled "Nick the Greek" types who play every hand on the theory that any two cards can win — a losing strategy that luck can nonetheless reward for weeks, convincing its practitioners they're brilliant. In poker, working backward from outcomes is treacherous, because hidden information and luck stand between choice and result. Life — careers, investments, relationships, health — has poker's structure, not chess's.

This is not just a card player's intuition. John von Neumann, the polymath behind game theory, built the field explicitly on poker. In Theory of Games and Economic Behavior (1944, with Oskar Morgenstern), he formalized decision-making among agents with hidden information and competing interests. He dismissed chess as a mere form of computation with a theoretically correct move in every position. "Real life consists of bluffing, of little tactics of deception," von Neumann said — real games turn on asking what the other person thinks you will do.

Duke's own path embodies the frame. A cognitive psychology PhD candidate at Penn studying learning under Lila Gleitman, she left academia when illness interrupted her studies, took up poker to pay bills, and spent two decades as a professional — winning a World Series of Poker bracelet and over $4 million — treating the table as a live laboratory in decision-making under uncertainty.
Your Brain Was Built for Certainty, Not Accuracy

Why is resulting so hard to shake? Because our cognitive machinery evolved for a world that rewarded speed and certainty, not statistical nuance. An ancestor who heard a rustle and instantly assumed "predator" outlived the one who paused to weigh base rates. We are pattern-seekers wired to impose order, to prefer confident narratives over probabilistic ones.

Daniel Kahneman's framework from Thinking, Fast and Slow names the machinery: System 1 is the fast, automatic, reflexive mind — pattern recognition, snap judgments, emotion; System 2 is the slow, effortful, deliberative mind that can do probability and self-correction. Duke prefers the labels reflexive mind and deliberative mind. The problem is capacity and laziness: System 2 is a small, easily exhausted resource, while System 1 makes the overwhelming majority of our judgments — including our verdicts on past decisions. Most irrationality lives in the gap between what our deliberative mind endorses and what our reflexive mind actually executes.

Crucially, Duke rejects the fantasy of simply "using System 2 more." Nobody can deliberate their way through life's thousands of daily choices; a poker player facing a decision every two minutes for hours certainly can't. The realistic goal is to train better habits into the reflexive mind — the strategy the rest of the book builds out.

Hindsight bias compounds the problem. Once we know how something turned out, the outcome feels as if it were inevitable and foreseeable all along — knew-it-all-along thinking. After Butler's interception, the pass call looked obviously doomed, and the 98% of futures in which it worked became invisible. Hindsight bias quietly rewrites uncertain decisions into certain ones, which is precisely why judging by results feels so natural and is so misleading.
Redefining "Wrong": Every Decision Is a Bet

If outcomes can't certify decisions, the words "right" and "wrong" need rebuilding. Duke's move: treat every decision as a bet on an uncertain future — a commitment of resources to one possible course among many, each with some probability of paying off. (Chapter 2 develops the betting frame in full; here it does the work of redefinition.)

Under that frame, being "wrong" is not the same as losing. If a forecaster says there's a 76% chance of rain and the sun shines, she wasn't wrong — the 24% branch simply occurred. If Carroll's play had a ~98% chance of a fine result, the interception doesn't convict him. Symmetrically, a good outcome doesn't acquit a bad decision: the drunk driver who makes it home safely still decided terribly. Outcomes are drawn from distributions; single draws certify nothing.

This reframing also changes what improvement means. Since the quality of our lives is the product of just two things — the quality of our decisions and luck — and only the first is in our control, the aim is to get better at separating them and steadily upgrading the controllable part. Perfection isn't on offer. Poker taught Duke that the winners aren't the players who are always right; they're the players who are slightly better calibrated than their opponents, hand after hand. In any domain ruled by uncertainty, a small edge in decision quality, applied repeatedly, compounds into an enormous long-run advantage (chapter 3 quantifies this).

It even lowers the emotional temperature of mistakes. When "wrong" no longer means "the outcome was bad," admitting error stops being an identity threat and becomes a probability update — I had the odds at 70/30; the evidence says 40/60. That shift is what makes the rest of the book's practices psychologically possible.
The Power of "I'm Not Sure"

The chapter's foundation stone is a phrase most professionals are trained to avoid: I'm not sure. Duke found that when she answered strategy questions this way — even "what's the best starting hand?" gets "it depends" — audiences initially heard evasion or incompetence. We equate confidence with competence and treat expressed uncertainty as weakness.

But "I'm not sure" is usually the most accurate answer available, and accuracy is the whole game. The world is genuinely stochastic and our information genuinely incomplete; pretending otherwise doesn't remove the uncertainty, it just hides it from the person who most needs to see it. An expert's edge isn't the elimination of doubt — it's a better-calibrated map of it: knowing whether the odds are 52/48 or 90/10 when everyone else just says "definitely."

Embracing uncertainty pays concrete dividends. It inoculates against black-and-white thinking, where anything short of 100% certainty gets rounded to ignorance and one contrary data point "disproves" a claim. It makes us better learners, because a person who holds beliefs provisionally updates them when the cards turn over, while a person who "knew" must defend or deny. And it makes us fairer judges of ourselves and others, because it keeps the luck term visible in every outcome we evaluate.

Duke closes the chapter where the book will spend the rest of its pages: getting comfortable with not being sure is step one. Everything that follows — vetting beliefs with "wanna bet?", fielding outcomes into luck and skill buckets, recruiting truthseeking groups, and time-traveling around our impulses — is technique built on this single stance toward the world. Life is poker. Play it like a player, not like a fan judging the highlight reel.
In practice
Catch yourself resulting this week: when you label a choice "good" or "bad," ask whether you are grading the decision process or just its outcome.
Re-examine your last painful outcome and write down what you actually knew at decision time — the information, the alternatives, the rough odds — before passing judgment on it.
Replace "I was right/wrong" with probability language: "I had that at about 70%, and the 30% happened."
Say "I'm not sure" out loud at least once in a professional setting this week, then follow it with your best-calibrated estimate instead of false confidence.
Before your next significant decision, list the plausible outcomes and assign rough probabilities, so hindsight can't later pretend the result was inevitable.
Identify one recent good outcome that followed a sloppy process, and one bad outcome that followed a sound one — proof in your own life that the two come apart.
